elements.sent-2411-001: We present a new iterative deblending method to separate the host galaxy (HG) and their Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) emission with the use of Integral Field spectroscopic (IFS) data.
elements.sent-2411-002: The method decomposes the resolved HG emission from the unresolved AGN emission by modelling the two-dimensional surface brightness (SB) profile of the point-spread function (PSF) and the two-dimensional SB HG continuum simultaneously per each monochromatic slide.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our method does not require any prior information about the observed SB profile or a detailed fitting of the PSF, making it ideal for the automatic analysis of large galaxy samples.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we test the quality of our method, its advantages, and its disadvantages.
elements.sent-2411-005: We test our method by using a set of IFS mock data cubes to quantify the reliability of our deblending process and further compare our method with the {\sc QDeblend3D analysis tool.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we applied our method to three data cubes selected from the MaNGA survey according to the dominance of either its HG or its AGN.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that our deblending method is capable of disengaging the bright, nonresolved AGN emission from the HG continuum and its narrow emission lines.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, the decoupling depends on how well the IFS spatially resolves the PSF, and on the relative flux intensity of the HG-AGN.
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, the method is ideal for disentangling the bright-flux contribution from AGN-dominated spectra.
elements.sent-2411-010: Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) manifest in very different ways in the Universe, from those that hide within seemingly normal galaxies to those whose vast amounts of energy are known to far outshine the light of the stars in their host galaxies (HG).
elements.sent-2411-011: This situation immediately leads to questions about the AGN link with the HG and how the AGN energy feedback impacts their evolution.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, the energy of radio jets emerging from the AGN can heat the interstellar medium and quench the formation of stars in the HG \citep[e.g.,][]{McNamara+00,Bruggen+02,Forman+07,Barai+16,Gaspari+20,Husemann+22 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, the intense energy of the AGN can ionise the surroundings of the HG interstellar medium up to kiloparsec scales and form the extended emission line regions and the extended narrow line regions \citep[EELR,ENLR,][]{Stockton+83,Unger+87,Heckman+91,Garcia-Lorenzo+2005,Bolton+07,Sanchez+2007c,Eilers+17,Villar-Martin+18,Chen+19,Husemann+22 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, it is not completely clear how these processes interact in the complex mechanisms governing the evolution of the HG.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, how the galaxy environment connects to their nuclear activity, or how the dynamical times of the galaxy are connected with the short and intense energy outburst cycles of their AGN are open questions \citep[e.g.,][]{Peng+10,Hickox+14 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Therefore, studying the HG, their evolution, and their properties is important, particularly during the most energetic phases of their AGN \citep{Lammers+23 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The luminosity of an AGN can easily outshine the emission of its HG, depending on the type of AGN \citep{Antonucci+93,Villarroel+14, Cortes+22, Jalan+23 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Consequently, it is essential to distinguish the HG emission from the intense AGN contribution.
elements.sent-2411-019: A major obstacle in image and spectroscopic studies is the high contrast between the AGN emission and its HG, which is usually two to three times fainter than the AGN flux.
elements.sent-2411-020: To address this issue, several approaches based on AGN/HG spatial deconvolution, decomposition, and deblending have been developed from imaging and spectra data.
elements.sent-2411-021: These approaches emphasise the importance of having reliable decomposition methods to separate the spectrum of the central AGN from its HG as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-022: One of these approaches was developed by \citet[][]{Magain+98 , who implemented a robust deconvolution algorithm that avoids deconvolution artefacts and can be used to retrieve foreground galaxy lensing maps from the residuals \citep[e.g.,][]{Richardson+72,Lucy+74,Skilling+84,Courbin+97,Courbin+98,Burud+98 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Furthermore, \citet{Courbin+2000 presented the spectroscopic version of the \citet[][]{Magain+98 algorithm.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: They demonstrated that clean HG spectra of objects with severely mixed AGN-HG objects can be accurately extracted.
elements.sent-2411-025: Many observational studies have used visible imaging to investigate the EELRs of the HG \citep{Kristian+73,Gehren+84,Malkan+84,MMG+84,Smith+86,Hutchings+87,Romanishin+89,Veron-Cetty+90,McLeod+94 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In addition, several works have tried to study the AGN and their HG galaxies properties by decoupling the AGN emission from their HG \citep[e.g.,][]{Bennert+2011,Bennert+2021,Kim+2008,Bruce+2016,Kim+2017,Kim+2019,Li+2021,Li+2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: They model the surface brightness (SB) profiles of the AGN non-resolved emission and the extended HG emission using the tool {\sc galfit \citep{Peng02 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Those works show that the HG can be modelled with a \citet{Sersic1963 profile, exploring the implication of 2D modelling of the bulge and disk with different S\'ersic indexes.}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, all these works indicate the importance of an accurate model of the SB profile of the AGN emission as a point-spread function (PSF).
elements.sent-2411-030: For example, with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) capabilities, \citet{Li+2021 use the foreground stars to obtain an accurate PSF model to subtract the AGN contribution from the HG.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Therefore, for the AGN-HG decomposition, the accurate 2D modelling of the SB significantly impacts the decomposition's success.
elements.sent-2411-032: During the last decade, Integral Field Spectroscopy (IFS) has significantly improved the way of studying galaxy properties.
elements.sent-2411-033: The IFS provides a way to spatially resolve the spectral properties of galaxies: the resolved ionised gas, the kinematics, and its stellar properties \citep[e.g.,][]{Cappellari+2011,Perez+2013,Ibarra-Medel+16,Tissera+16,Cano-Diaz+16,Cano-Diaz+22,Gonzalez-Delgado+15,Gonzalez-Delgado+2016,Gonzalez-Delgado+2017,Lopez-Fernandez+18,Sanchez+2020,Aquino+2020,Artemi+2022,Ibarra-Medel+22b .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Furthermore, the use of IFS in astronomy has provided the advent of large IFS surveys of galaxies such as the Calar Alto Legacy Integral Field Area survey \citep[CALIFA,][]{Sanchez+2012 , the Sydney-AAO Multi-object Integral field spectrograph survey \citep[SAMI,][]{Croom+2012 , the Mapping Nearby Galaxies at the Apache Point Observatory survey \citep[MANGA,][]{Bundy+2015 , which is part of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey phase IV \citep[SDSS-IV,][]{Abdurro+22 , and more recently the upcoming Local Volume Mapper from SDSS phase V \citep[LVM,][]{Almeida+23 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Other works explored the point-spread function (PSF) deconvolution to study the kinematic properties of galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-036: In this direction, \citet{Chung+21 presented an alternative PSF deconvolution algorithm based on the Lucy-Richardson algorithm \citep{Lucy+74,Richardson+72 .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: They used IFS data from MaNGA and generated a set of IFS mock observations to demonstrate that the algorithm can recover the true stellar kinematics.
elements.sent-2411-038: Consequently, the IFS offers an unparalleled opportunity to analyse the spatially resolved characteristics of the ionised gas and the HG's extended emission-line regions (EELRs).
elements.sent-2411-039: One of the first AGN-deblended approaches with IFS was presented by \citet{Sanchez+2004a and \citet{Garcia-Lorenzo+2005 , they present an adapted version of {\sc galfit \citep{Peng02 called {\sc galfit3d \citep{Sanchez+2006c .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: With this tool, they studied the central region of 3C 120 and disentangled the AGN emission using IFS data and HST WFPC imaging.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this study, they separated the AGN emission from the EELR and detected six structures associated with a previous merger event and interactions with the radio jet observed in the central region of 3C 120.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, the implementation of {\sc galfit3d implies the use of {\sc galfit to model the HG and the AGN surface brightness in many wavelength slides of the data cube, requiring a field star to model the PSF.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Later, \citet{Christensen+06 used IFS data to study the AGN and its HG EELRs.}
elements.sent-2411-044: They discussed three techniques for subtracting AGN emissions.
elements.sent-2411-045: The first approach involves using synthetic, adjustable narrow-band filters to observe a set of emission lines and measure the adjacent continuum, thus allowing the nuclear emission to be removed and narrow-band images of the HG EELRs to be produced.
elements.sent-2411-046: The second is an iterative method that assumes that the spatially resolved spectrum of a point source (AGN) is the same across adjacent spaxels but scaled by flux.
elements.sent-2411-047: To begin, they combined the central AGN spectrum within a 2-arcsecond aperture.
elements.sent-2411-048: With this extracted spectrum, they calculated the scale factor of the individual spaxels at the loci of an intense emission line to create a new data cube.
elements.sent-2411-049: This data cube contains the central extracted spectrum but is scaled spaxel by spaxel.
elements.sent-2411-050: Then, they subtracted this data cube from the original data to obtain a residual data cube.
elements.sent-2411-051: This residual data cube is again removed from the original cube to get the AGN data cube \citep{Sanchez+2004a .}
elements.sent-2411-052: This process is repeated three times to obtain a pure EELR data cube.
elements.sent-2411-053: The third method involves implementing a two-dimensional fitting of the PSF to model the AGN spectrum for each spectral sampling or monochromatic slice.
elements.sent-2411-054: This technique was previously implemented by \citet{Wisotzki+03 and later improved by \citep[][]{Sanchez+04,Sanchez+2004a,Sanchez+06,Sanchez+2007c,Mendez-Abreu+2017,Mendez-Abreu+2019,Mehrgan+2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The approach they used is based on the assumption that the full-width half maximum (FWHM) of the PSF changes smoothly across the wavelength dispersion.
elements.sent-2411-056: Then, the PSF model is interpolated and subtracted to recover an extended emission-line region (EELR) data cube.
elements.sent-2411-057: Therefore, they used the AGN cube to recover a data cube from the EELR.
elements.sent-2411-058: Later, \citet{Husemann+13 continued to improve and develop their deblending algorithm, and finally they presented it as the {\sc QDeblend3D}\footnote{\url{https://github.com/brandherd/QDeblend3D and \url{http://www.bhusemann-astro.org/?q=qdeblend3d software tool \citep{Husemann+14 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: More recently, \citet{Husemann+22 improved their deblending algorithm to implement a wavelength dependency on the PSF.}
elements.sent-2411-060: They interpolate the PSF variations from specific spectral windows.
elements.sent-2411-061: However, {\sc QDeblend3D still needs prior information of the HG/AGN relative fluxes to perform the PSF model to subtract and remove the AGN contribution from the HG emission.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Hence, a wrong HG/AGN relative flux value will return to a bad AGN/HG decomposition, implying the need for a previous 2D surface brightness modeling using external tools such as {\sc galfit .}
elements.sent-2411-063: On the other hand, \citet{Mendez-Abreu+2019 and in their successive articles apply the 2D surface brightness modeling to spectroscopically disentangle the structural properties (bar, disk and bulge) of galaxies observed with IFS.}
elements.sent-2411-064: However, the \citet{Mendez-Abreu+2019 methodology is not intended to perform an AGN/HG decoupling but to spectroscopically decouple the bar, disk and bulge.}
elements.sent-2411-065: With the advent of the James Web Space Telescope (JWST), several groups have been working on a method to provide a way to decouple the non-resolved AGN emission from the resolved HG emission using the IFS data of the JWST \citep[e.g.,][the Q3D group]{Wylezalek+2022, Veilleux+2023,Vayner+2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: They created the tool named {\sc q3dfit}\footnote{\url{https://q3d.github.io/ that, in a similar way as {\sc galfit3d , it requires to previously have an accurate model of the JWST PSF to subtract the AGN emission from the HG emission.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: We present a new implementation of AGN-HG decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-068: The presented method is a new iterative method designed for IFS data that models the two-dimensional AGN point source and the two-dimensional HG continuum at the same time in each monochromatic slide.
elements.sent-2411-069: This method focuses on AGN-dominated spectra and aims to decouple the resolved HG spectra that are completely buried on the strong non-resolved AGN emission.
elements.sent-2411-070: The principal difference from previous methods is that we designed our methodology to not depend on prior information of the 2D surface brightness PSF and the HG profiles.
elements.sent-2411-071: Our methodology does not need to model a foreground star or the HG previously to get the PSF profiles.
elements.sent-2411-072: In addition, our method does not require interpolating the PSF between different spectral regions.
elements.sent-2411-073: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-074: In Section \ref{sec:method , we describe, in a detailed form, the workflow of our deblending method, the inputs and its outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In Section \ref{sec:mocks , we test the precision of our method by implementing a set of IFS mocks that simulate the resolved emission of a set of AGN.}
elements.sent-2411-076: In Section \ref{sec:realdata , we implement our method using three IFS MaNGA galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-077: In that section, we disentangle the AGN/HG spectra, analyse the emission lines of the deblended AGN/HG spectra, and present a characterisation of their nuclear activity and its black-hole masses.
elements.sent-2411-078: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:summary , we review the advantages and drawbacks of the method.}

The boxes indicate the main process of the method: The AGN-HG decomposition. elements.sent-2411-079: The methodology underlying this article has been implemented in a code written in python3 that is publicly available in a GitHub repository called {\sc AGNdecompose}\footnote{\url{https://github.com/hjibarram/AGN_decompose .}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The method uses a 2D surface brightness decomposition to extract the AGN spectrum from the HG spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-081: The decomposition models the SB of the observed galaxy as the contribution of two profiles: a \citet{Sersic1963 profile for an extended contribution (HG) and a \citet{Moffat+1969 profile for the unresolved contribution (AGN).}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The works of \citet{Kim+2008 demonstrated that using a single Sersic profile is sufficient to decompose the AGN emission from the host.}
elements.sent-2411-083: However, we plan for future development to include the \citet{Mendez-Abreu+2019 methodology to include more complex modelling of the 2D surface brightness of the HG.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The methodology workflow for our 2D AGN-HG decomposition is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:diagram .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Our method works in a similar fashion to {\sc galfit3d \citep{Garcia-Lorenzo+2005 but with the difference that we consider a PSF that varies with the wavelength, as we describe below.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Also, our method does not require an accurate model of the PSF with a foreground star as it is needed for {\sc galfit3d and {\sc q3dfit .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: This method has the advantage of being applied in an automatic form without the need for external feedback and any additional modelling.
elements.sent-2411-088: Hence, one of the main objectives of this work is to explore and quantify the inaccuracies in the AGN-HG deblending by characterizing the PSF with only the AGN emission on IFS observations.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Our method: The AGN-HG spectra decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-089: We model the non-resolved AGN contribution as a point source with a PSF SB profile that follows a Moffat \citep{Moffat+1969,Trujillo+2001 function with the following form: \begin{equation}F_{psf}(i,j)=A_t\times\left(1+\frac{R(i,j)^2}{\alpha^2}\right)^{-\beta}.\label{EQ1}\end{equation} In this case, $A_t$ is the peak value of the PSF profile, $x_0$ and $y_0$ are the central positions of the profile, $\alpha$ is the dispersion and $\beta$ traces the size of the extended wings of the PSF.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Mathematically, as $\beta$ increases, the profile asymptotically tends to be a Gaussian profile \citep{Trujillo+2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The value of $R(i,j)$ is the distance from the PSF centered to its isophotal value.
elements.sent-2411-092: For an ideal case, the isophotal can have a circular shape; however, our model allows us to define an elliptical shape by defining $R(I,j)$ as: \begin{equation}\begin{split}\mathbf{ R(i,j)=\sqrt{\frac{(1-e^2\sin^2[\arctan(\Delta_x,\Delta_y)-\theta])\times(\Delta_x^2+\Delta_y^2)}{1-e^2}}} \mathbf{\Delta_x=i-x_0} \mathbf{\Delta_y=j-y_0} \label{Eq:Radi} \end{split}\end{equation} Where $e$ and $\theta$ are the PSF isofotal's ellipticity and positional angle.
elements.sent-2411-093: If $e$ is zero, i.e., a circular isophotal shape, the Equation \ref{Eq:Radi returns to the standard definition of a circular radius: $R(i,j)=\sqrt{(i-x_0)^2+(j-y_0)^2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: If $e$ is not zero, $R(i,j)$ returns the mayor axis of the elliptical isophotal.
elements.sent-2411-095: The method has the option to fix $e$ and $\theta$, or level them as an extra of two variables to fit.
elements.sent-2411-096: For the extended spatially resolved source, we use the classical definition of a \citet{Sersic1963 profile defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} F_{ext}(i,j)=I_o\times exp \left\{ -b_n\left[ \left(\frac{R'(i,j)}{R_e}\right)^{1/n_s}-1\right] \right\} R'(i,j)=\sqrt{(i-x_0)^2+(j-y_0)^2}. \end{split}\end{equation} Where $I_o$ and $R_e$ are the values of the half-light intensity and radius, and $n$ is the S\'ersic index.}
elements.sent-2411-097: In this case, for simplicity, we assume a circular isophotal shape.
elements.sent-2411-098: For the aim of this method, a perfect model of the extended emission is not needed; we require an educated guess of the extended background to fit the intense central emission of the AGNs.
elements.sent-2411-099: However, in a future release of the method, we plan to implement more complex models, including disks, bars and asymmetries.
elements.sent-2411-100: Therefore, the model fits the observed SB profile as $F_{psf +F_{ext}$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: We set the central positions $x_0$ and $y_0$ as the same positions for $F_{psf}$ and $F_{ext}$.
elements.sent-2411-102: The total number of free parameters for the non-constrain mode (see below) are nine (eleven): $At$, $x_0$, $y_0$, $\alpha$, $\beta$, $I_e$, $b_n$, $R_e$, and $n_s$ ($\mathbf{e}$, $\mathbf{\theta}$).
elements.sent-2411-103: To obtain the best value for the free parameters, we use the \citet{emcee implementation of the Affine Invariant Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) Ensemble Sampler with the use of the python package {\sc emcee}\footnote{https://emcee.readthedocs.io/en/stable/ .}}
elements.sent-2411-104: With the SB modelling, the AGN/HG decomposition works as follows (see left panel of Figure~\ref{fig:diagram ):.}
elements.sent-2411-105: SB fit within spectral window slides: We divide the spectral range into a set of spectral slides of ten spectral pixels each.
elements.sent-2411-106: For each slide, we integrate the spectral information to boost the SNR SB map by $\sqrt{10}$ times.
elements.sent-2411-107: For the case of MaNGA, this is equivalent to a window of 15 \AA\ since MaNGA has a spectral sampling of 1.5 \AA\ per spectral pixel.
elements.sent-2411-108: Then, we perform a non-constraint 2D SB modelling of $F_{psf +F_{ext}$ for each slide and obtain the best-fit values of $A_t$, $x_0$, $y_0$, $\alpha$, $\beta$, $I_o$, $b_n$, $R_e$, and $n_s$ ($\mathbf{e}$, $\mathbf{\theta}$).}
elements.sent-2411-109: Therefore, we obtain a low-resolution spectral dependence for each of the nine (eleven) variables.
elements.sent-2411-110: Fixing the S\'ersic parameters: From the previous step, we obtain their low-resolution smoothing spectral response for only the $b_n$, $n_s$, and $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-111: The method first smooths each spectral response function with a Gaussian kernel of the size of two times the spectral window used in step a).
elements.sent-2411-112: Then, the method applies a linear interpolation of the values at the full spectral sampling.
elements.sent-2411-113: If the PSF isophotal is not circular, the variables $e$ and $\theta$ are also interpolated to the full spectral range.
elements.sent-2411-114: The method exploits the assumption that the host S\'ersic parameters $b_n$, $n_s$, and the PSF parameters $\beta$ ($e$, $\theta$) cannot change drastically as a function of wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-115: In other words, those parameters can only monotonically change as a function of wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-116: SB fit for all spectral sampling: Once we obtain the interpolated values of $b_n$, $n_s$, and $\beta$ ($e$, $\theta$), we proceed to re-fit the SB profile but now at each spectral sampling or monochromatic slide (one spectral pixel).
elements.sent-2411-117: We are able to reduce our space parameter from nine (eleven) to six: $A_t$, $x_0$, $y_0$, $\alpha$, $I_o$, and $R_e$.
elements.sent-2411-118: The code has a set of keywords to interpolate $R_e$, $I_0$, $x_0$, and $y_0$ values, leaving only $A_t$ and $\alpha$ as free parameters.
elements.sent-2411-119: However, those keywords are not activated as default values.
elements.sent-2411-120: Reconstruction of the nonresolved point source (AGN) spectral model: We reconstruct the point source spectra using the full spectral responses of $A_t$, $x_0$, $y_0$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$ ($\mathbf{e}$, $\mathbf{\theta}$).
elements.sent-2411-121: We use Equation~\ref{EQ1 to recover the flux map at each spectral pixel and then reconstruct the AGN spectral cube.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The parameters $I_o$ and $R_e$ are the only free parameters to model the HG S\'ersic profile.
elements.sent-2411-123: Retrieve the AGN and HG spectra: With the AGN model cube, we simply obtain the residual cube (Original Cube minus AGN Cube).
elements.sent-2411-124: The residual cube will contain the extended emission associated with the HG that includes the HG EELR.
elements.sent-2411-125: On the other hand, the AGN cube will contain the point source unresolved emission: the AGN spectra including the Broad Emission Lines (BELs), their Narrow Components (NC), and the nonthermal continuum.
elements.sent-2411-126: We finally obtain the spectral responses of the nonresolved emission: $A_t(\lambda)$, $\alpha(\lambda)$, $\beta(\lambda)$, $x_0(\lambda)$, $y_0(\lambda)$, and $e(\lambda)$, $\theta(\lambda)$ if the PSF elliptical isofotal is activated.
elements.sent-2411-127: With this information, we proceed to extract the pure unresolved (AGN) emission flux with the next analytical expression: \begin{equation}\begin{split} F_{AGN}(\lambda)=\int F_{psf}(r,\lambda)dr=\frac{\pi \alpha(\lambda) ^2 A_t(\lambda)}{\beta(\lambda)-1}. \end{split} \label{eq-fluxtot}\end{equation} This expression comes from the integral of Equation \ref{EQ1 or the total flux of the PSF 2D SB profile at each spectral sampling.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Therefore, the method is able to obtain the total non-resolved flux spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-129: In addition, the method produces an AGN-free HG cube.
elements.sent-2411-130: The user can apply diverse analysis tools to study the HG AGN-free properties and the AGN HG-free spectra.
elements.sent-2411-131: This paper implements the {\sc pyPipe3D and the {\sc specfit (IRAF's task)-specific tools to retrieve the stellar, the HG EELR, and the AGN BEL, NC and the nonthermal continuum proprieties.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: This marks the end of our AGN-HG decomposition methodology.
elements.sent-2411-133: Next, we describe the methodologies used to analyze the disentangling HG and the AGN spectra, remarking that those methods are no longer part of our AGN/HG decomposition method.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, in this work, we use {\sc pyPipe3D and {\sc specfit to quantify the accuracy of our blending method.}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: The HG SSP and emission line analysis.
elements.sent-2411-135: For the HG analysis, we use the {\sc pyPipe3D}\footnote{\url{https://gitlab.com/pipe3d/pyPipe3D/ analysis tools.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: With the residual data cube, we proceed to model the stellar spectra.
elements.sent-2411-137: We follow the next steps (see the middle panel of Figure \ref{fig:diagram ):.}
elements.sent-2411-138: The single stellar population (SSP) fitting: We use the {\sc pyPipe3D spectral fitting tool.}
elements.sent-2411-139: It works as follows: It performs a spatial segmentation to achieve an SNR of 50.
elements.sent-2411-140: Then, {\sc pyPipe3D runs a first model of the stellar spectra per each spatial bin and obtains a gas spectrum from which it fits the strong emission lines.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Then {\sc pyPipe3D runs an SSP analysis using a full stellar library.}
elements.sent-2411-142: In this case, we use the MaSTAR-sLOG stellar library defined in \citet{Sanchez+2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-143: It uses 273 SSP templates that cover 39 stellar ages (from 1 Myr to 13.5 Gyr) and seventh metallicities ($Z_{\star =$, 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.002, 0.008, 0.017, 0.03, 0.04).}
elements.sent-2411-144: This library uses a \citet{Salpeter+55 initial mass function (IMF).}
elements.sent-2411-145: In addition, {\sc pyPipe3D uses a \citet{Cardelli+1989 dust extinction law for internal extinction.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Following the SSP fitting, {\sc pyPipe3D reconstructs a set of 2D maps that contains the properties of the HG stellar population properties.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Finally, {\sc pyPipe3D obtains a gas cube by subtracting the stellar model cube from the HG residual cube.}
elements.sent-2411-148: The gas cube will contain the total emission of the HG pure EELR.
elements.sent-2411-149: The emission line fitting is fully described in \citet{Lacerda+2022 and \citet{Sanchez+2022 , but we summarize the process.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: With the gas cube, {\sc pyPipe3D implements a spaxel by spaxel fit of the emission lines.}
elements.sent-2411-151: This set includes the \ha,\ \hb,\ \niill\ and \oiiiopt\ lines.
elements.sent-2411-152: The emission line fit is done following the next workflow: {\sc pyPipe3D implements a random method (called RND) based on a Monte-Carlo (MC) $\chi^2$ minimization to retrieve the best model parameters (amplitude, velocity dispersion and the velocity shifts).}
elements.sent-2411-153: Once this step is done, {\sc pyPipe3D uses the best-fit parameters of the MC as prior value for a Levenberg-–Marquardt method to re-explore the space parameter and find the best model fit of each emission line.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Therefore, {\sc pyPipe3D will return a set of spatially resolved maps of the fluxes, velocity dispersion, velocity shifts, and equivalent widths.}
elements.sent-2411-155: To analyze the recovered AGN spectra, we use the {\sc IRAF-specfit task \citep{kriss94 .}}
elements.sent-2411-157: The user must select a combination of functional forms to model all the emission/absorption line components, starting from an underlying continuum constructed by an initial flux guess and a power law index.
elements.sent-2411-158: Then, {\sc specfit model the strong BELs followed by the NCs and other additional components such as the well-known \feii\ optical pseudo continuum and absorption lines, if needed.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The task also permitted us to choose Gaussian or Lorentzian line profiles given as input of their intensity, central wavelength, and FWHM.
elements.sent-2411-160: An ASCII file should be provided with the initial guesses of the selected components.
elements.sent-2411-161: This selection allows us to model complex line systems such as blended emission and absorption lines, absorption edges, and complex continuum features such as the \feii\ emission and extinction.
elements.sent-2411-162: All emission components linearly contribute to modelling the input spectra.
elements.sent-2411-163: The fitting is carried out by a $\chi^2$ minimization using a Marquardt, Gridfit or Alternate algorithms taken from the Numerical recipes \citep{Press+1986, kriss94 .}
elements.sent-2411-164: The minimization ended when the tolerance requirements met the input specification or reached the maximum number of iterations.
elements.sent-2411-165: Finally, the best-fitting parameters for each spectral component are saved in a log file.
elements.sent-2411-166: Another file for plotting could also be provided with all flux components as functions of the wavelength.

elements.sent-2411-167: To estimate the bias and uncertainties of our deblending process, we generate a set of six mock IFU observations with mock AGN emission spectra.
elements.sent-2411-168: To generate those mocks, we used a set of three MaNGA \citep{Bundy+2015 galaxies: MaNGA-IDs 1-211017, 1-38348, and 1-458316, see Figure~\ref{fig:MANGA .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: The three galaxies have strong EELRs without the presence of any AGN activity.
elements.sent-2411-170: Therefore, the selection of these galaxies is ideal to be the input of HG-resolved spectra for the mocks.
elements.sent-2411-171: The galaxy 1-211017 is a 127 MaNGA IFU fiber bundle (32" in diameter) to test a high spatial resolution IFS observation.
elements.sent-2411-172: The galaxy 1-38348 is a 37 IFU bundle (17" in diameter) to test the intermediate spatial resolution IFS.
elements.sent-2411-173: Finally, the galaxy 1-458316 is a 19 IFU bundle (12" in diameter) to test the low spatial resolution IFS.
elements.sent-2411-174: Assuming that the MaNGA FOV covers at least 1.5 effective radii ($R_e$), the difference among the 127, 37 and 19 bundles implies a spatial resolution of 0.05, 0.09 and 0.13 $R_e$ per spaxel.
elements.sent-2411-175: MaNGA has a spaxel size of 0.5" and a fiber diameter of 2.5" \citep{Law+2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-176: To generate the AGN mock spectra, we first obtain the composite spectra of 1000 luminous quasars from the SDSS-DR16th quasar catalog \citep{SDSSDR17Q .}
elements.sent-2411-177: To construct the composite spectra, we follow the methodology described in \citet{Vanden+2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-178: This methodology normalizes the set of quasar spectra at a rest-frame wavelength window that is common among all the spectra and obtains the average.
elements.sent-2411-179: The composite spectra are then redshifted to the rest-frames of the three MaNGA galaxies, and it is used to reproduce the total AGN flux emission ($F_{AGN}$) of the mocks.
elements.sent-2411-180: We define the value of the Moffat FWHM PSF size from the analytical description in Equation 1 of \citet{Trujillo+2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-181: This analytical description returns the dependence of the PSF size as a function of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-182: Then, we set a wavelength dependency of the PSF as a monotonic function that changes from 2.5 arcsec at 3,500 $\angstrom$ to 3 arcsec at 11,500 $\angstrom$.
elements.sent-2411-183: Also, we define that $\beta$ should be a constant value of 26 dex.
elements.sent-2411-184: The PSF properties are based on observed MaNGA PSF profiles taken from stars of the MaNGA Stellar library program \citep{Yan+2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-185: We fit the 2D PSF SB profiles of the MaNGA stars IFS observations, to obtain a realistic MaNGA PSF profiles as a functions of wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-186: Therefore, we can provide an accurate PSF ($\alpha$) variation based on actual MaNGA IFS data.
elements.sent-2411-187: With the value of the PSF and $\beta$, we can solve the value of $\alpha$ from Equation 1 of \citet{Trujillo+2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-188: Then, we use our Equation \ref{eq-fluxtot to get the value of $A_t$ as a function of $\alpha$ and $\beta$.}
elements.sent-2411-189: We define a monotonic shift of the AGN centroid ($x_0, y_0$) from the centre of the MaNGA IFU Field-of-View (FoV) at 3,500 $\angstrom$ to 0.5 arcsec at 11,500 $\angstrom$ in the north to south direction.
elements.sent-2411-190: Finally, with the values of $A_t(\lambda)$, $\beta(\lambda)$, $\alpha(\lambda)$, $x_0(\lambda)$, and $y_0(\lambda)$, we can use our Equation \ref{EQ1 to obtain the 2D AGN spectra within the MaNGA IFU FoV.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The final IFU mock spectra are the sum of the 2D HG spectra with the 2D AGN spectra.
elements.sent-2411-192: In this step, we rescale the total flux of the 2D AGN spectra to be 1.2 and 3.2 times the total flux of the HG spectra within 5,100 to 5,300 $\angstrom$.
elements.sent-2411-193: These values can represent the intermediate AGN-HG spectra and the AGN-dominated spectra.
elements.sent-2411-194: Therefore, we obtained six mock IFS observations: three spatial-resolution IFS observations (highR, intR, lowR) with intermediate and dominated (Int, Dom) AGN spectra each.

elements.sent-2411-195: With the six mock IFU spectra, we apply our AGN-HG decomposition algorithm and compare the deblended output HG and AGN spectra with the input HG and AGN spectra.
elements.sent-2411-196: With the input and output spectra, we calculate the flux residuals of the recovered decomposed spectra, defined as \begin{equation}\Delta Flux =(Flux_{out}-Flux_{in})/Flux_{tot},\end{equation} where $Flux_{out}$ is the recovered AGN or HG spectra, $Flux_{in}$ is the input AGN or HG spectra and $Flux_{tot}$ is the total contribution of the combined HG and AGN spectra.
elements.sent-2411-197: Therefore, $\Delta Flux$ is the residual between the input and output spectra normalised by the total observed flux of the spectra.
elements.sent-2411-198: In addition, by normalizing the spectra by $Flux_{tot}$, the absolute flux residuals of the recovered AGN and the recovered HG are the same.
elements.sent-2411-199: As we explain in Section \ref{sec:method , the recovered HG spectrum is the residual of the original spectra minus the recovered AGN spectrum.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Therefore, the sum of the recovered HG plus the recovered AGN spectra will always be the input spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-201: A summary of the average normalised residuals is given in percentages in Table \ref{tab:presicion_percentages .}
elements.sent-2411-202: In the next subsections, we describe our results about the AGN/HG decomposition of the spectra continuum, the deblendind of the emission lines of both the HG and the AGN spectra, and the recovering of the point-spread function as a function of the wavelength.

elements.sent-2411-217: In addition, the normalised error is below the values of the AGN-HG deblended residual.

elements.sent-2411-222: Low-resolution quasar-dominated resolved spectra (LowR-Dom): In this case, we explore an IFS observation with low spatial resolution with an AGN spectrum flux 3.2 times stronger than the HG flux.
elements.sent-2411-223: We show in the upper panels of Figure \ref{fig:low_resolution the results of this case.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Our deblended method is capable of deblending the AGN spectrum with a normalised residual of $15\%$ of the total flux greater than 5,000 $\angstrom$, and below that wavelength the residual increases to $5\%$ at 4,000 $\angstrom$.
elements.sent-2411-225: Low-resolution intermediate quasar resolved spectra (LowR-Int): For this case, we present the results for a low spatial resolution IFS observation where the AGN spectrum flux is not dominant.
elements.sent-2411-226: We show these results in the lower panels of Figure \ref{fig:low_resolution .}
elements.sent-2411-227: The normalised residual of the deblended AGN spectrum is on the order of $30\%$ above 6,000 $\angstrom$.

elements.sent-2411-230: To perform this comparison, we model and fit the HG EELR and the AGN BAL and NC from the input and output spectra for each mock case.
elements.sent-2411-231: For that aim, we use the {\sc pyPipe3D analysis tool to fit the spatially resolved \ha, \hb, \niill, \siill\ and \oiiia\ HG emission lines, and the {\sc IRAF-specfit to fit the AGN \ha, \hb, \niill, \siill\ and \oiiia\ NCs and the \hb\ and \ha\ BELs.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: With the emission line model, we obtain each line's total flux, velocity shift and the FWHM.
elements.sent-2411-233: Hence, we can compare how well our deblended method is able to recover those emission line properties.
elements.sent-2411-234: We define the parameter \begin{equation}\Delta val=\frac{|val_{in}-val_{out}|}{val_{in}},\end{equation} where $val_{in}$ represents the total flux, velocity shift or FWHM values estimated from the input HG or AGN spectra, and $val_{out}$ represents the value estimated from the deblended recovered spectra.
elements.sent-2411-235: Therefore, the parameter $\Delta val$ represents the absolute value of the normalised residual between the input and output values.
elements.sent-2411-236: Since estimating the input and output parameters uses the same fitting methodology and the same fitting constraints, $\Delta val$ indicates the absolute deviation of the recovered emission line properties from the deblended spectra compared with the true values.
elements.sent-2411-237: We describe the results for the HG EELR, AGN BEL, and NCs.
elements.sent-2411-238: The HG EELR: We use the spatially resolved emission line values provided by {\sc pyPipe3D to obtain the $\Delta val$ of the flux, velocity shifts and FWHM of \ha, \hb, \niill, \siill\ and \oiiia\ emission lines.}
elements.sent-2411-239: In the left part of Figure~\ref{fig:ELMock shows how the \ha\ $\Delta val$ is spatially resolved at different resolutions for the AGN dominated cases.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Figure~\ref{fig:ELMockE shows the flux average $\Delta val$ values integrated within the central 3'', which is the equivalent of the SDSS fiber diameter.}
elements.sent-2411-241: The central flux values contain the larger residual of the HG EELR parameters.
elements.sent-2411-242: However, we see a small dependency on the spatial resolution of the IFS: for the HighR-Dom and HighR-Int cases, the flux $\Delta val$ ($\Delta flux$) for \ha, \hb\ and \siill\ are between the $6\%$ to $9\%$ of the input flux, with the \niill\ around $13\%$ and \oiiia\ around $30\%$ of the input fluxes.
elements.sent-2411-243: However, for the IntR-Dom and IntR-Int cases, all the lines have a $\Delta flux$ around $30\%$, and for LowR-Dom and LowR-Int, we obtain a $\Delta flux$ around $40\%$.
elements.sent-2411-244: For the velocity shift ($\Delta vel$), we cannot find a dependency with the IFS spatial resolution, but we find a much lower $\Delta val$ than the flux case.
elements.sent-2411-245: For the HighR-Dom and HigR-Int cases, we find a $\Delta vel$ around $8\%$.
elements.sent-2411-246: In contrast, the IntR-Dom and IntR-Int recuperate a $\Delta vel$ around $30\%$, and the LowR-Dom and LowR-Int obtain a value around $8\%$.
elements.sent-2411-247: Finally, we also find a weak dependency with the IFS spatial resolution for the FWHM $\Delta val$ ($\Delta FWHM$).
elements.sent-2411-248: For the HighR-Dom and HigR-Int cases, the $\Delta FWHM$ values are between $7\%$ to $9\%$, for the IntR-Dom, IntR-Int, LowR-Dom and LowR-Int cases the cases the value of $\Delta FWHM$ are around $11\%$ to $15\%$.
elements.sent-2411-249: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ELMock , the central region of the galaxy is the most affected by the AGN emission.}
elements.sent-2411-250: However, we note that the larger the spatial resolution, we obtain a lower deviation of the true values is extended.
elements.sent-2411-251: This result is expected since the larger spatial resolution implies a small relative size of the PSF FWHM compared to the IFS spatial sampling.
elements.sent-2411-252: It is also important to note the asymmetries of the residuals on the $\Delta flux$ for all the cases.
elements.sent-2411-253: This result shows the method's difficulty in dealing with asymmetries when modelling the HG SB.
elements.sent-2411-254: We plan to improve our method by adding more sophisticated models that include bar and disk structures.
elements.sent-2411-255: The maps also show that the velocity shift and the line FWHM are well recuperated within a more confined region.
elements.sent-2411-256: In addition, as Figure~\ref{fig:ELMockE already shows, the Intermediate resolution case is the one that has the highest $\Delta vel$ of the other two cases.}
elements.sent-2411-257: The high residual value of $\Delta vel$ can also be related to the HG SB.
elements.sent-2411-258: The AGN BEL and NC fitting: We use the {\sc IRAF-specfit routine \citep{kriss94 to fit the input and output AGN spectra.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: The spectral fitting considers the peculiarities in the shape of the input AGN line profiles.
elements.sent-2411-260: We consider the emitting region that comprises the \hb\ region, including the \oiiiopt\ doublet and the emission of the pseudo continuum of \feii.
elements.sent-2411-261: For the AGN input spectra (see Section \ref{sec:Mock IFU Spectra ), it is of particular importance the \feii\ emission, which can affect the estimation of the emission from the red wing of \hb\ \citep[e.g.][]{Kovavcevic+2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-262: A wide window with intense \feii\ multiplet emission is required to constrain it adequately.
elements.sent-2411-263: We chose the window 4450-5400 \AA\ and the \feii\ template used in \citet{negrete2018 , which adequately reproduces the \feii\ emission around \hb.}
elements.sent-2411-264: We tested two fits considering a Gaussian and a Lorentzian profile for the broad \hb\ component to choose the one that met the requirements seen in the diagnostic diagram for quasars in the optical \citep[e.g.][figures 1 and 3 to 8]{negrete2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-265: For a FWHM < 4000 km s$^{-1}$ for the BC of \hb, a Lorentzian profile should be used.
elements.sent-2411-266: The choice of this profile is also supported by the moderate \feii\ emission and returns the best fit with a lower $\chi^2$ than using a Gaussian profile.
elements.sent-2411-267: For the remaining components, the \hb\ narrow component and the \oiiiopt\ doublet, we use Gaussian profiles with the same shift and FWHM.
elements.sent-2411-268: As reported in \citet{negrete2018 , we considered a second component for \oiiiopt\ with a free shift and FWHM on the basis that it is a high ionization line and is susceptible to showing blue asymmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-269: In the \ha\ region, we perform a spectral fitting in the 6200-6850 \AA\ range that comprises the intense broad \ha\ fitted with the same line profile as \hb, and the narrow components \niill\ and \siill\ fitted again with Gaussian profiles, sharing the same shift and FWHM.
elements.sent-2411-270: For both \ha\ and \hb\ fits, we consider the same (within the errors) continuum fitted with a single power law.
elements.sent-2411-271: Once all the components were considered, we made the fitting with {\sc IRAF-specfit via $\chi^2$ minimization using a Marquardt algorithm \citep{kriss94 .}}
elements.sent-2411-272: We show the results on the right part of Figure~\ref{fig:ELMockE .}
elements.sent-2411-273: Because of the intense emission of the AGN BELs, the $\Delta flux$ of the \ha\ and \hb\ broad components have the lowest residuals as expected with values around $0.01\%$ to $0.1\%$.
elements.sent-2411-274: For the case of the $\Delta vel$ of the broad components, we find a wide range of residuals, however they are almost negligible in comparison with the HG EELR counterparts, with values around $0.01\%$ to $4\%$.
elements.sent-2411-275: For the $\Delta FWHM$ of the broad components, we find a value range between $0.01\%$ to $9\%$, with \ha\ being more consistent with a residual value around $0.01\%$ and \hb\ with a value around $9\%$.
elements.sent-2411-276: In any case, we cannot find any dependency on the IFS resolution or the dominance of the AGN-HG emission.
elements.sent-2411-277: For the case of the NCs, we find that the $\Delta flux$ residuals have values around $0.3\%$.
elements.sent-2411-278: Specifically, for the HighR-Dom and HighR-Int cases, \ha NC has the largest residuals around $1\%$ and \hb NC the lowest residuals around $0.08\%$ with \oiiiopt, \niill\ and \siill\ scattered between those values.
elements.sent-2411-279: For the IntR-Dom and IntR-Int cases, all lines have a $\Delta flux$ residual around $0.3\%$; and for LowR-Dom and LowR-Int, the residual values are scattered between $0.01\%$ and $1\%$ with \ha\ and \hb\ NCs having the lowest residuals.
elements.sent-2411-280: Similarly, we cannot find any dependency on the IFS resolution or the dominance of the AGN-HG emission.
elements.sent-2411-281: However, for the case of $\Delta vel$, we have a weak dependency on the AGN intensity.
elements.sent-2411-282: We measure a small residual value for the AGN-dominated cases compared to the AGN intermediate case.
elements.sent-2411-283: We cannot find any clear dependency with the residuals for the spatial resolution.
elements.sent-2411-284: Overall, $\Delta vel$ is around $0.3\%$ for all the lines with a range from $0.1\%$ to $1\%$.
elements.sent-2411-285: Finally, for the $\Delta FWHM$ residual, we have a more consistent value among all the mock cases without any clear dependency.
elements.sent-2411-286: The \ha NC, \niill, and \siill\ emission lines have the lowest residuals around $0.2\%$, with \hb NC and \oiiiopt being the ones with the largest residual around $1\%$.

The small panels below each principal panel show the normalised residuals: The {\sc QDeblend3D} (red) and our deblendig spectra (blue) minus the input AGN spectra over the input AGN spectra. The spectra are the total flux within 2.5 arcsecs of the centre of the IFU-FoV.} \label{fig:qdblendMock}
elements.sent-2411-287: The residuals in the deblending decreases as the AGN flux is more intense with respect to the HG flux.
elements.sent-2411-288: In all the cases, the residuals always decreases at lower wavelengths, where the nonthermal power-law continuum of the AGN increases and blurs the flux of the HG.
elements.sent-2411-289: This effect is also seen in the BELs, where the total flux of the AGN is larger than the HG.
elements.sent-2411-290: On the other hand, the IFS spatial resolution has an impact on the deblending of the HG EELRs: the larger the spatial resolution, the better the deblending of the HG emission lines.
elements.sent-2411-291: In this work, we define the spatial resolution as the relative size of the FWHM of the PSF in comparison with the spaxel size: in other words, the spatial resolution is the minimum size on an IFS from which it is possible to resolve two different structures, that differs from the spatial sampling (the spaxel size).
elements.sent-2411-292: Therefore, our method is sensitive to the total resolution within the IFS FoV.
elements.sent-2411-293: It is important to note that when the spatial resolution decreases, the FoV of the IFS in proportion to the FWHM size of the PSF also decreases.
elements.sent-2411-294: This behaviour is due to the fact that (at least for homogeneous IFS surveys) the FWHM of the PSF remains stable for all the IFS observations: the instrument is always at the same observatory.
elements.sent-2411-295: Therefore, there is an error in the estimation of the PSF Moffat wings when the resolution of the IFS observation changes.
elements.sent-2411-296: To explore this issue, we measure how the FWHM size of the recuperated PSF changes relative to the input values for all mock cases.
elements.sent-2411-297: In this point, we use the definition of the Moffat FWHM PSF profile of \citet{Trujillo+2001 , that is $FWHM_{PSF =2\alpha\sqrt{2^{1/\beta -1}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-298: We can define the normalised FWHM PSF residual size as: \begin{equation}PSF_{frac}=\frac{FWHM_{PSF,in}-FWHM_{PSF,out}}{FWHM_{PSF,in}},\end{equation} where $FWHM_{PSF,in}$ is the input FWHM size of the PSF used to create the mock AGN spectra as described in Section~\ref{sec:Mock IFU Spectra , and $FWHM_{PSF,out}$ is the output FWHM size of the PSF of the deblended AGN spectra.}
elements.sent-2411-299: We show the values of $PSF_{frac}$ along the wavelength range for all six mock cases in Figure \ref{fig:psf_deltas .}
elements.sent-2411-300: For all the cases where the AGN flux spectrum domains over the HG spectrum (HighR-Dom, IntR-Dom, and LowR-Dom ), the residuals of the recovered FWHM size of the PSF is within $5\%$ below 8,500 $\angstrom$.
elements.sent-2411-301: Above that wavelength, the errors associated with the imperfect subtraction of the skylines (on the original IFS observations) start to degrade the PSF modelling.
elements.sent-2411-302: As a consequence, the final FWHM size of the PSF starts to depart from the input values.
elements.sent-2411-303: On the other hand, for the IntR-Int, and LowR-Int cases, the residuals of the deblended PSF is within $15\%$.
elements.sent-2411-304: The results indicate that the relative flux between the non-resolved and the resolved emission (AGN/HG) has a larger impact on accurately modelling the PSF profile.
elements.sent-2411-305: This is expected because the larger the contribution of the AGN is, the easier it is for the method to model the PSF.
elements.sent-2411-306: However, only for the HighR-Int case, the residuals are also within $5\%$, indicating that when a sufficient spatial resolution is available, it is also possible for the method to model the PSF profile accurately even if the AGN emission is not too bright.
elements.sent-2411-307: So far, we have described the method performance to recuperate the input FWHM of the non-resolved PSF SB.
elements.sent-2411-308: However, the FWHM only traces the recovering performance of the input $\alpha$ and $\beta$ parameters \citep{Trujillo+2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-309: To explore the recovering performance of the PSF centroid ($x_0$ and $y_0$), we define the next smoothed normalised residuals: \begin{equation}x_{0,frac}=\frac{x_{0,in}-x_{0,out}}{x_{0,in}}, y_{0,frac}=\frac{y_{0,in}-y_{0,out}}{y_{0,in}},\end{equation} where $x_{0,out}$ and $y_{0,out}$ are the recovered and smoothed spectral values of $x_{0}$ and $y_{0}$.
elements.sent-2411-310: In Figure~\ref{fig:xy_deltas , we show the results for all our mock cases.}
elements.sent-2411-311: The normalised residuals show that $x_0$ and $y_0$ are the best-recuperated parameters of the PSF profile, whatever the mock case.
elements.sent-2411-312: For the HighR-Dom, IntR-Dom and IntR-Int cases, the normalised residuals are below $0.5\%$, especially for $y_0$, where the normalised residuals are below $0.3\%$.
elements.sent-2411-313: For the HighR-Int case, the maximum residual values are below $0.7\%$ for $x_0$ and below $0.3\%$ below $y_0$.
elements.sent-2411-314: Finally, for the LowR-Dom and LowR-Int, the maximum residual values reach $1\%$ and $2.5\%$ for each case for the $x_0$ and $y_0$ parameters.
elements.sent-2411-315: Therefore, while the centroid of the PSF is the best-recuperated parameter, there is a dependence on the spatial resolution: a large spatial resolution returns a lower normalised residual.
elements.subsection-2411-006: The BPT Diagram on the deblended HG spectra
elements.sent-2411-316: We now explore the results of the deblending of the HG EELRs returned by our method.
elements.sent-2411-317: For this case, we use the Baldwin, Philips \& Terlevich NII diagram \citep{Baldwin+81 .}
elements.sent-2411-318: The BPT diagram is one of the most known narrow-line diagnostic diagrams in the literature \citep[e.g.,][]{Veilleux+87,Kewley+01,Kauffmann+2003May .}
elements.sent-2411-319: This diagram is used to disentangle the ionisation properties of the nebular gas that can be associated with AGN emission, star formation, and aged stars.
elements.sent-2411-320: Therefore, the BPT diagram is ideal for testing the AGN/HG deblending of the EELRs.
elements.sent-2411-321: In this exploration, due to the size of the FoV of the low-resolution cases (LowR-Dom, LowR-Int), we use only the deblending results for the high- and intermediate-resolution cases.
elements.sent-2411-322: In Figure \ref{fig:BPTMock we show the BPT diagrams before and after the deblending process.}
elements.sent-2411-323: For this analysis, we use {\sc pyPipe3D to analyse the nebular emission lines for the original and HG+AGN data cubes of our three galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-324: It is important to note that {\sc pyPipe3D is not designed to analyse broad emission line spectra; however, it performs a well-suitable continuum subtraction to analyse the EELRs.}
elements.sent-2411-325: Therefore, we can use the nebular line analysis of {\sc pyPipe3D to generate the BTP diagram of the non-deblended spectra only for comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-326: In addition, to construct the spatially resolved BPT diagram, we use only the spaxels that have an SNR greater than 5.
elements.sent-2411-327: For the case of the non-deblended HighR-Dom, the resolved BPT shows intense contamination due to the AGN emission within the central 5 arcsec.
elements.sent-2411-328: The central spaxels show an emission narrow line ratios originating from an AGN ionisation source: the line ratios lie above the \citet{Kewley+01 demarcation line.}
elements.sent-2411-329: In addition, the middle spaxels (beyond 5 arcsecs) lie below the Kewley demarcation line but above the \citet{Kauffmann+2003May line.}
elements.sent-2411-330: The Kauffmann and Kewley lines define the composite region, where it is not completely clear if the main ionisation source comes from an AGN or a star formation region.
elements.sent-2411-331: On the other hand, the spatially resolved BPT diagram suffers a radical change when the AGN emission is deblended.
elements.sent-2411-332: After removing the nonresolved AGN emission, almost all the line ratios of the HG EELRs lie below the Kauffmann demarcation line.
elements.sent-2411-333: This result shows that the only ionisation sources are from star formation regions, as expected from the input mocks.
elements.sent-2411-334: For the non-deblended HighR-Int case, we obtain that most of the central spaxels lie in the BPT composite region.
elements.sent-2411-335: However, after applying the HG/AGN deblending algorithm, most of the spaxels are in the star-forming region.
elements.sent-2411-336: Once we compare with the input values of the mock host galaxies (before adding the AGN spectra), the method can recover the true host emission line ratios, with only a small number of spaxels slightly crossing the Kauffman line.
elements.sent-2411-337: For the non-deblended IntR-Dom and the IntR-Int cases, we obtain a very similar BPT spaxel distribution as the HighR-Dom case, with most of the central spaxels lying above the Kewley line or within the composite region of the BPT.
elements.sent-2411-338: On the contrary, the BPT diagram estimated from the deblended HG spectra shows a completely different behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-339: Most of the central spaxels lie in a very well-defined region located between the BPT composite region and the star-forming region.
elements.sent-2411-340: Once we compare the line ratios of the input HG, we show that the BPT spaxel positions of our deblended HG spectra are the same as the input HG emission line ratios.
elements.sent-2411-341: To visualise the spatially resolved AGN deblending across the BPT, we present in Figure \ref{fig:map_example_rgb the Red-\niib, Green-\ha, and Blue-\oiiia~ in an RGB image of the input, the PSF model, and the residual map for the AGN/HG dominant cases.}
elements.sent-2411-342: We also show the input HG map.
elements.sent-2411-343: For the HighR-Dom case, the method can spatially disentangle the nonresolved AGN emission from the resolved HG EELR.
elements.sent-2411-344: In its residual, it is clearly shown that most of the HG EELR is located on the disk of the galaxy, which agrees to be associated with SF regions.
elements.sent-2411-345: In a similar way, we find a similar result for the case of the IntR-Dom case, with the HG EELR clearly decoupled from the nonresolved AGN emission.
elements.sent-2411-346: Also, Figure \ref{fig:map_example_rgb shows that the HG \ha~ emission is stronger than the \niib~ emission, as we show in Figure \ref{fig:BPTMock .}}
elements.sent-2411-347: Finally, for the LowR-Dom case, we show that most of the \ha~emission comes from the AGN emission, as we also show in Fig. \ref{fig:low_resolution .}
elements.sent-2411-348: In this section, we compare our deblending methodology with the one presented by the {\sc QDeblend3D quasar deblending code presented by \citet{Husemann+13 and \citet{Husemann+22 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-349: We decided not to compare our method with {\sc galfit3d or {\sc q3dfit because those methods require a prior characterization of the PSF profile, making a crucial difference with our method.}}
elements.sent-2411-350: On the other hand, {\sc QDeblend3D does not perform a prior PSF modelling, but it requires initial information on the relative flux of the AGN/HG.}
elements.sent-2411-351: Therefore, the closest comparison of our method is with {\sc QDeblend3D .}
elements.sent-2411-352: We use only the HighR mocks because they have the best decomposition results: any deviation of the deblended spectra from the input spectra will be caused only by the deblending procedure and not by any limitation due to the IFS observation and PSF/FOV sampling.
elements.sent-2411-353: Figure \ref{fig:qdblendMock shows the deblending comparisons for the HighR-Dom and HighR-Int cases.}
elements.sent-2411-354: For both cases, {\sc QDeblend3D recovers with great detail the AGN continuum above 5,000 \AA\ and maintains a constant precision of $\approx$ 10$\%$ of the flux for the HighR-Dom case and $\approx$ 20$\%$ for the HighR-Int case.}
elements.sent-2411-355: This precision is comparable with the recovered precision of our method between 5,000 \AA\ and 7,000 \AA.
elements.sent-2411-356: However, wavelengths below 5,000 \AA\ {\sc QDeblend3D tend to return a bluer spectrum, increasing (or making it constant) the residual precision to 20$\%$.}
elements.sent-2411-357: For wavelengths above 7,000 \AA\, the {\sc QDeblend3D precision tends to be constant, while our methodology varies depending on the spectra error and the sky residuals.}
elements.sent-2411-358: In addition, our method tends to return lower residuals for the same wavelength range.
elements.sent-2411-359: It is important to say that in the loci of the strong emission lines (\ha\ and \hb), while the residuals of the BEL are smaller in our method, the residuals of the NC are smaller and best recuperated by {\sc QDeblend3D .}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/Age_L_prof.png} \caption{Radial profiles of the Age$_{LW}$ for the high-resolution (upper panel) and intermediate-resolution (lower panel) cases. The black solid lines represent the radial profiles of the input MaNGA galaxies without the simulated AGN spectra. The red solid lines represent the radial profiles of the HG-deblended spectra for the quasar-dominated case. The segmented blue lines represent the radial profiles of the HG-deblended spectra for the quasar intermediate case. The yellow solid line represents the FWHM PSF size.} \label{fig:ages}
elements.sent-2411-360: In this section, we explore the recovery of the SSP properties from the deblended HG spectra during phase two of our method.
elements.sent-2411-361: In particular, we explore the light-weighted (LW) SSP Ages ($Age_{LW}$) radial profiles.
elements.sent-2411-362: We will present a detailed analysis of the SSP properties in a forthcoming paper.
elements.sent-2411-363: For this test, we use the mock IFS cubes considered above.
elements.sent-2411-364: We select the $Age_{LW}$ because it is the most sensitive SSP property with respect to the slope of the spectra.
elements.sent-2411-365: Hence, any bias with respect to the HG deblending will directly impact the estimation of $Age_{LW}$.
elements.sent-2411-366: The definition of $Age_{LW}$ is described in detail in Equation 7 of \citet{Ibarra-Medel+22b .}
elements.sent-2411-367: However, we briefly define $Age_{LW}$ as the average of the SSP Ages weighted by the SSP spectral flux within a certain spectral window.
elements.sent-2411-368: In this case, we define the spectral window within 4,500 to 5,500 $\angstrom$.
elements.sent-2411-369: To obtain the original LW Ages, we apply {\sc pyPipe3D to the input MaNGA cubes before the construction of the mock data cubes.}
elements.sent-2411-370: This step returns an SSP synthesis without the existence of a central bright AGN emission.
elements.sent-2411-371: We use the SSP analysis of the respective mock HG deblended spectra.
elements.sent-2411-372: Then, we obtain a direct comparison of the input SSPs (original data cube) with the deblended version of the same galaxy (deblended HG).
elements.sent-2411-373: For this test, we use only the HighR and IntR cases.
elements.sent-2411-374: The low spatial resolution of the LowR case cannot be used to obtain a good representation of the real Age profiles due to the size of the PSF, as we explored in \citet{Ibarra-Medel:2019aa .}
elements.sent-2411-375: In Figure \ref{fig:ages , we show the results of these two cases.}
elements.sent-2411-376: For the HighR case, we find that the central Age values match those values of the input galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-377: For values larger than 0.6 $R_{50}$, the estimated Ages are 0.1 dex older than the original values.
elements.sent-2411-378: However, this difference is between the Age error estimations.
elements.sent-2411-379: For the IntR case, the SSP Ages of the central values return to be $\approx$ 0.2 dex younger than the Ages of the input galaxy, and then, at larger radii, the values tend to be older by $\approx$ 0.1 dex.
elements.sent-2411-380: Again, these differences are within the Age errors.
elements.sent-2411-381: For all of the cases, we find that the intensity of the AGN does not affect the SSP analysis of the deblended HG spectra.
elements.sent-2411-382: Finally, it is important to note that a bias may exist in the SSP decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-383: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:qdblendMock , the residuals show that {\sc QDeblend3D returns a more flattened spectral residual slope in contrast with our method.}}
elements.sent-2411-384: In addition, \citet{Sarmiento+2023 showed that the extinction parameter is mainly affected by the spectral slope.}
elements.sent-2411-385: Therefore, the recovered dust extinction from the SSP decomposition can be biased to lower values for our deblended HG spectra.
elements.sent-2411-386: However, the age values are less affected by the spectral slope due to the SSP modelling, which also considers the absorption features, remaining within 0.2 dex of the true values, as shown in \citet{Sarmiento+2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-387: The selection of the input host galaxy spectra 1-211017 (HighR), 1-38348 (IntR) and 1-458316 (LowR) were chosen for their star formation activity and the condition they do not have any evident nuclear activity.
elements.sent-2411-388: However, they were not selected based on their morphological types.
elements.sent-2411-389: Their morphologies are Sb, SBb and SAB0a for 1-211017, 1-38348, and 1-458316, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-390: The morphologies were taken from the MaNGA Visual Morphologies from SDSS and DESI images Value Added Catalog\footnote{\url{https://www.sdss4.org/dr17/data_access/value-added-catalogs/?vac_id=manga-visual-morphologies-from-sdss-and-desi-images (Vazquez-Mata et al., in prep).}}
elements.sent-2411-391: In addition, 1-458316 is a spheroid-like galaxy with a faint bar structure; see Figure~\ref{fig:MANGA .}
elements.sent-2411-392: The morphologies of the input HG for the HighR and LowR galaxies present structures that can not be well-modelled by a simple S\'ersic profile.
elements.sent-2411-393: For the LowR case, a simple S\'ersic profile is more suitable due to its morphology.
elements.sent-2411-394: On the other hand, the main objective of our decoupling method is to disentangle the non-resolved (AGN) from the resolved (HG) flux by modelling the SB.
elements.sent-2411-395: To model the non-resolved flux, we require a background value to fit the PSF parameters.
elements.sent-2411-396: In addition, the actual spatial resolution and the field of view (FOV) of an IFS observation do not reach the values required that different morphologies could impact the performance of the method.
elements.sent-2411-397: Therefore, assuming a S\'ersic profile to model the resolved SB is a good option.
elements.sent-2411-398: The results confirm this: the best decoupling results are for the HighR, a typical Sb galaxy with complex spiral arm structures that cannot be well modelled with a simple S\'ersic profile.
elements.sent-2411-399: We obtain the largest residuals for the LowR case when a simple S\'ersic profile is more suitable due to its morphology.
elements.sent-2411-400: In addition, we will improve the code in a future version to model more complex structures of the host SB to deal with the upcoming high-spatial resolution IFS observations.
elements.sent-2411-401: Finally, we check the case when the $R_e$ and $I_0$ are set to be interpolated during the full spectral modelling phase of our method.
elements.sent-2411-402: We obtain that the fitting spectral response of $R_e$ presents small fluctuations per wavelength slide.
elements.sent-2411-403: Those fluctuations are originated by very small changes of the SB fitting due to subtle noise level changes on the data and the stochastic behavior of an MCMC fitting.
elements.sent-2411-404: However, those fluctuations per spectral pixel are strongly reduced with the interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-405: On the other hand, $I_o$ principally traces the host galaxy spectra of all the emissions and absorptions not associated with the AGN emission, and it is more affected by the original noise from the original spectra.
elements.sent-2411-406: However, as discussed in the previous section, the decoupling of the non-resolved spectra is not affected since the method only uses the HG model as a background value, and the final recovered HG spectrum is simply the residual of the original spectra minus the deblended PSF spectra.
