elements.subsection-2411-011: Deblending of the host emission lines: The BPT
elements.sent-2411-438: Similarly, as in Section \ref{Mock_BPT , we explore the deblending of the HG EELRs returned by the method by exploring their BPT diagrams.}
elements.sent-2411-439: For all the galaxies, we compare how the BPT diagrams change with and without the AGN/HG deblending process.
elements.sent-2411-440: We use the outputs of the emission line fitting from {\sc pyPipe3D and generate the BPTs before and after the deblending process; see Figure \ref{fig:bpt .}}
elements.sent-2411-441: The BPT diagrams from the non-deblended galaxy IFUs show that the narrow line ratios of the central spaxels (two arcseconds) have line ratios characteristic of AGNs above the \citet{Kewley+01 line.}
elements.sent-2411-442: On the other hand, the line ratios from the deblend HG spectra of the same galaxies show different properties.
elements.sent-2411-443: The central spaxels move mostly from above to below the Kewley line.
elements.sent-2411-444: For the case of the MaNGA galaxy 1-200510 and 1-235576, the average central spaxels are now within the composite region.
elements.sent-2411-445: Finally, for 1-210017, we find the most dramatic change between the unblended/deblended BPT diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-446: For the unblended case, the central spaxels are clearly within the AGN areas, whereas for the host deblended case, the central spaxels are between the composite and the star formation areas.
elements.subsection-2411-012: Deblending of the AGN emission lines
elements.sent-2411-447: With the pure AGN deblending spectra, we now proceed to model the \hb (blue) and \ha (red) spectral regions.
elements.sent-2411-448: We use the spectral line fitting from the {\sc IRAF-specfit routine \citep{kriss94 .}}
elements.sent-2411-449: The {\sc specfit routine has the advantage that it can model the underlying continuum and the emission lines at the same time.}
elements.sent-2411-450: The spectral regions were delimited as follows.
elements.sent-2411-451: The blue one within 4,500-5,400 $\angstrom$ includes the emission lines \hb, \oiiiopt, the \feii\ template, \heiiopt, and \ni.
elements.sent-2411-452: The red one within 6,200-6,800 $\angstrom$ includes \ha, \niill, \siill, and \oill.
elements.sent-2411-453: For all permitted lines, we consider both broad and narrow components.
elements.sent-2411-454: In the case of \oiiiopt, in two objects, it was necessary to add a second semi-broad component.
elements.sent-2411-455: As \oiiiopt\ is a high-ionisation line, this second component has been associated with outflows \citep[e.g.][]{negrete2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-456: In both regions, we fit the non-thermal continuum using a nearly flat power law.
elements.sent-2411-457: For all emission lines (except for \feii), we use Gaussian profiles with the following input parameters: a central lambda, the line intensity and the FWHM.
elements.sent-2411-458: We fit the \feii\ emission around \hb\ considering the template by \cite{marziani2009 , which is based on the spectrum of I Zw 1 \citep[see also][]{negrete2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-459: In Figure \ref{fig:specfit , we show the blue and red spectral regions and their models.}
elements.sent-2411-460: We started the fit in the red region, starting with the strong broad lines and then adding the narrow ones.
elements.sent-2411-461: For \oiiiopt\ (both narrow and semi broad), \niill\ and \oill, we consider the theoretical ratio 3:1 for its line fluxes \citep{Osterbrock+2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-462: We assume that all narrow components come from the same region and thus have the same FWHM shift.
elements.sent-2411-463: Then, we used the \ha\ model as the basis to model \hb.
elements.sent-2411-464: In addition, we model a possible absorption line component to double-check if there is any HG residual.
elements.sent-2411-465: For the case of the MaNGA 1-200510 galaxy, the fitting of the deblended AGN BLR returns a double broad component for both Balmer lines, one blue-shifted and the other red-shifted, with respect to the systemic restframe.
elements.sent-2411-466: Looking at the residuals, we saw it is necessary to add an additional broader blue-shifted component, much fainter than the first ones.
elements.sent-2411-467: The FWHM of these three broad components are 3,400$\pm$100 and 3,500$\pm$700 \kms\ for the blue components; 2,700$\pm$90 and 2,740$\pm$540 \kms\ for the red components; and 9,950$\pm$700 and 8,500$\pm$300 \kms\ for the broader blue-shifted component in \ha\ and \hb, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-468: The FWHM of the narrow components is 300$\pm$30 \kms\ for both \ha\ and \hb\ regions.
elements.sent-2411-469: The FWHM of the \oiiiopt\ semi-broad component is 1400$\pm$200 \kms.
elements.sent-2411-470: On the other hand, for MaNGA 1-235576, we find that the BLR can be modelled with a single broad component with FWHM of 2,700$\pm$100 and 3,100$\pm$100 \kms, plus a narrow component with FWHM of 300$\pm$30 and 500$\pm$50 \kms, for \ha\ and \hb, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-471: The best fit for the MaNGA 1-210017 galaxy is similar to 1-200510.
elements.sent-2411-472: It returns a double broad component.
elements.sent-2411-473: We find FWHM values of 3,400$\pm$100 and 3,300$\pm$200 \kms\ for the blue components, and 3400$\pm$100 and 4200$\pm$500 \kms\ for the red components, for \ha\ and \hb, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-474: The FWHM of all narrow components is 400$\pm$40 \kms.
elements.sent-2411-475: It is important to note the similarity between \hb\ and \ha\ broad and narrow emission lines for the three cases.
elements.sent-2411-476: The physical interpretation of the modelled double broad components can be associated with the signature of the kinematics of the accretion disk as reported by, e.g., \citet[][]{Storchi-Bergmann2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-477: The double broad components with FWHM up to a few tens of thousands \kms\ are common in low-luminosity AGN and radio galaxies, usually at low-$z$, and can be easily recognized by the top flatted broad component line profile or, in some cases, both broad lines are visually separated.
elements.sent-2411-478: The suggested scenario is the continuity between the inner part of the broad-line region (BLR) and the outer part of the accretion disk.
elements.sent-2411-479: In the case of low-luminosity objects and objects with low Eddington ratio ($L_{bol /L_{Edd < 0.1$), such as radio galaxies, the outer material from the BLR can reach the inner part of the central machine, i.e., the accretion disk \citep[e.g.,][]{Eracleous1994,Netzer2013,Popovic2014 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-480: In addition, double BELs with different characteristics and asymmetries for AGNs have also been explained by the residual HG absorption lines contribution \citep[e.g.,][]{Bon2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-481: For the narrow components of the deblended AGN, we obtain the values of \niib/\ha\ and \oiiia/\hb\ line ratios.
elements.sent-2411-482: The measured line ratios indicate a clear ionisation AGN source in the BPT diagram.
elements.sent-2411-483: In Figure \ref{fig:bpt , we plot the positions of the line ratios on the deblended BPT diagrams.}
elements.sent-2411-484: Finally, we find that the modelled absorption lines are comparable with the dispersion of the residuals.
elements.sent-2411-485: Therefore, there is no evidence of the HG absorption features present in the deblended pure AGN spectra.
elements.sent-2411-486: This work presents a new alternative iterative method to perform a spatially resolved AGN deblending for IFS data.
elements.sent-2411-487: This method models the non-resolved AGN emission and the HG-resolved emission simultaneously in each monochromatic slide, being able to interpolate the spectral variability of the PSF across the wavelength range.
elements.sent-2411-488: This method is ideal for decoupling the intense emission from AGNs when the nuclear flux domains over the HG central region.
elements.sent-2411-489: We summarise the principal characteristics, results, and tests of the method:.
elements.sent-2411-490: The resolved/unresolved flux deblending: We explore the precision of the deblended algorithm by implementing a set of six mock IFS data cubes (three resolutions $\times$ two AGN dominances).
elements.sent-2411-491: Due to the two-dimensional surface brightness modelling, the HG deblending precision on the EELR depends on the effective spatial resolution of the IFS and the PSF size along the IFU FoV (Figs.. \ref{fig:high_resolution --\ref{fig:low_resolution ).}}
elements.sent-2411-492: The better the PSF is resolved, the better the method disentangles the narrow emission lines from the HG.
elements.sent-2411-493: In addition, the method performs better when the AGN flux is more intense.
elements.sent-2411-494: Our method uses the S\'ersic HG modelling as an initial background value to model the non-resolved AGN emission as a PSF profile.
elements.sent-2411-495: Therefore, the morphologies have little impact on the performance of our deblending method.
elements.sent-2411-496: For the mock cases, the HighR and IntR mocks, the input HGs present spiral and bar structures that can not be modelled with a simple S\'ersic profile.
elements.sent-2411-497: On the contrary, the LowR mock has a morphology that can be modelled with the S\'ersic profile.
elements.sent-2411-498: Still, the best decouple performance is for the HighR, which supports that the morphology has less impact on the deblending process.
elements.sent-2411-499: The method can perform a good deblending of the AGN and HG spectral continuum within $20\%$ of the normalised residual flux.
elements.sent-2411-500: In addition, we performed a detailed analysis of the deblending of the HG EELR and the AGN BEL and NC (Figs.
elements.sent-2411-501: The central HG EELR fluxes are deblended within $13\%$, $30\%$ and $40\%$ for the HighR, IntR and LowR spatial resolution cases, showing that our method is sensitive to the IFS spatial resolution.
elements.sent-2411-502: For the EELR velocity shifts, we recuperate the true central values within $30\%$ without an explicit dependency on the spatial resolution or the flux ratio between the AGN and the HG.
elements.sent-2411-503: In addition, for the case of the recovered FWHM of EELRs, the method can recuperate the true values within $15\%$.
elements.sent-2411-504: On the other hand, for the case of the AGN BEL and NC components, the method performs very well recuperating the true values within the $9\%$.
elements.sent-2411-505: The Deblended HG SSPs: With the mock IFS, we explore how well the methodology disentangles the HG spectra to estimate the SSP $Age_{LW}$.
elements.sent-2411-506: The SSP synthesis recovers the values of the central age once the estimation errors are considered.
elements.sent-2411-507: However, the central Age values tend to be slightly younger, and at larger radii, the Ages tend to be older but always within the error estimations (Fig. \ref{fig:ages ) Indeed, we need to be cautious in the interpretation of the SSP results due to any possible bias that could be propagated by the uncertainties of the AGH/HG decomposition.}
elements.sent-2411-508: Comparison with {\sc QDeblend3D : We compare our deblending method with the well-tested two-dimensional quasar deblending {\sc QDeblend3D tool.}}
elements.sent-2411-509: From the mock analysis, we find that both methods achieve a similar precision above 5000 \AA.
elements.sent-2411-510: However, {\sc QDeblend3D tends to produce a bluer AGN spectrum (or a redder HG spectrum) below 5000 \AA\ and maintains a stable residual above 7000 \AA.}
elements.sent-2411-511: In contrast, our method tends to produce lower residuals across the full spectral range.
elements.sent-2411-512: On the other hand, {\sc QDeblend3D recovers almost the same AGN continuum as that deblended by our method.}
elements.sent-2411-513: For the case where we did not have a perfect match with the output of {\sc QDeblend3D , we still have a good agreement with the AGN spectral continuum, with similar values of the normalised residuals (Fig. \ref{fig:qdblendMock ).}}
elements.sent-2411-514: Both tools perform similarly for emission lines, although {\sc QDeblend3D tends to overestimate the BEL regions, while depending on the object, the residuals of the NC regions tends to be better in {\sc QDeblend3D .}}
elements.sent-2411-515: Overall, the performance of both methods varies with spectral characteristics, likely due to limitations in flux input data, with each method returning a better decomposition on the NC regions depending on the object (Fig. \ref{fig:qdblend ).}
elements.sent-2411-516: The deblended HG EELRs from the BPT diagram: We measure the HG ionisation properties of the resolved emission lines of the spectra before and after applying our AGN/HG deblending method.
elements.sent-2411-517: We use the mock IFS observation and three real MaNGA data with ids 1-200510, 1-235576, and 1-210017.
elements.sent-2411-518: We found that the central emission line rations move on the NII-BPT diagram from the AGN ionisation region (before deblending) to the compose/SFR region (after deblending).
elements.sent-2411-519: Therefore, the deblending method can effectively disentangle the AGN emission from the HG emission.
elements.sent-2411-520: The deblended AGN emission lines profiles: With the three MaNGA cubes, we test the quality of the deblended AGN broad and narrow emission lines with a detailed fitting of the line profiles.
elements.sent-2411-521: We perform individual line modelling at the \hb\ and \ha\ complex and find that both regions can be fitted with a similar model.
elements.sent-2411-522: These models show that the BELs have a well-defined profile at each spectral region.
elements.sent-2411-523: This profile similarity is expected from a well-deblended AGN spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-524: In addition, we measure the position of the emission line ratios on the NII BPT diagram, confirming that the narrow lines have a clear AGN ionisation origin.
elements.sent-2411-525: For all previous tests, we show that the method is able to disentangle the intense central emission of their central AGN in IFS data.
elements.sent-2411-526: These tests show that our method can decouple the narrow nebular emission of the HG from the AGN emission and its stellar continuum, and provide pure host-free total quasar spectra.
elements.sent-2411-527: The advantage of this method is that it does not require any previous information on the PSF or HG SB profiles.
elements.sent-2411-528: The method models the PSF using the strong central AGN emission from the input IFS observation.
elements.sent-2411-529: However, this principle also came with a drawback: the model depends on how much information about the PSF can be retrieved from the IFS observation; how much the PSF is resolved within the IFS and how much it is the contrast of the non-resolved emission in comparison with the resolved HG emission.
elements.sent-2411-530: Therefore, the method depends on the spatial resolution of the PSF within the IFU FoV and the relative flux intensity of the AGN.
elements.sent-2411-531: In addition, there are inaccuracies with the HG 2D SB profile model, a drawback that we plan to attack in a future release of the method by implementing a more complex HG modelling that includes bars, discs and different asymmetries.
